---
title: "Using PLIDA: larger-than-memory data in R"
author: "Will Mackey"
---

::: {.callout-warning}
## Draft
This site is a work in progress. The code, benchmarks, and recommendations are still being tested and may change. Don't rely on anything here yet.
:::

## What this site is

A worked demonstration of modern R data-engineering tools — [arrow](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/r/), [duckdb](https://duckdb.org/docs/api/r) / [dbplyr](https://dbplyr.tidyverse.org/), [data.table](https://rdatatable.gitlab.io/data.table/), and base [dplyr](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/) — applied to PLIDA-style administrative microdata that is much larger than the machine's RAM. All code runs on a 32 GB MacBook against a ~750 GB synthetic PLIDA corpus produced by the [`fplida`](https://github.com/wfmackey/fplida) package.

The site answers four practical questions:

1.  Should CSVs be processed and stored as parquet before use?
2.  Is it worth building a DuckDB database from the parquet files first, or should we just query the parquet with views?
3.  Once the data is in place, which R idiom should you reach for — dplyr-on-tibbles, data.table, arrow, or dbplyr-over-DuckDB?
4.  How do we do all of this without crashing a 32 GB RAM machine?

## The short answer

If you read only the recommendations page: convert CSV to parquet with DuckDB, not Arrow — the local six-file benchmark put DuckDB at about 2x faster for that step, with slightly smaller output. Keep the parquet layout disciplined (`zstd`, medium row groups, medium-sized files), then load the parquet into a DuckDB database for the join-heavy and repeated work, and do everything else with `dbplyr` over that DuckDB connection. Fall back to `arrow::open_dataset` or DuckDB views over parquet for ad-hoc exploration where spinning up a database is overkill, and reach for `data.table` only after you have filtered down to something that fits comfortably in memory.

The rest of the site shows how we got there, with timings and memory footprints from real (synthetic) runs.

## How the pages fit together

- [The data](01-the-data.qmd) — what the fplida corpus looks like, which tables we use, and the join model.
- [CSV to parquet](02-csv-to-parquet.qmd) — how arrow streams a 44.5 GB CSV through a 2 GB memory footprint, and the cost/benefit of the conversion.
- [DuckDB](03-duckdb.qmd) — two ways to wire parquet up to DuckDB (views vs materialised tables), and the build cost of each.
- [Benchmarks](04-benchmarks.qmd) — five backends, three canonical tasks, timings and peak RSS.
- [Recommendations](05-recommendations.qmd) — the rule of thumb, with caveats.

## Reproducibility

All source data lives outside this repository at `/Users/willmackey/offline/fplida-data/fplida_30m_csv/` (synthetic, produced by `fplida`). All intermediate parquet and DuckDB files live at `/Users/willmackey/offline/using-plida-data/` so the project folder stays small. The scripts in `R/` are numbered in dependency order; `R/01-convert-csv-to-parquet.R` runs first, then `R/01b-convert-supplementary.R`, `R/02-build-duckdb.R`, and finally `R/04-run-benchmarks.R`.
